summaryText: The gray wolf, Canis lupus, is the largest wild member of the dog family and the ancestor of the domestic dog. It is a habitat generalist that once ranged across most of the Northern Hemisphere and still occupies parts of North America, Europe, and Asia, though its range is now fragmented. Wolves are obligate carnivores whose lives centre on hunting large hoofed prey such as deer, elk, and moose.

The African savanna elephant is the largest living land animal, but its scientific interest lies mostly in its behavior. It lives in a fluid, multi-tiered society organized around long-lived females, communicates partly below the range of human hearing, and carries a spatial and social memory that shapes how whole families survive on a changeable landscape. Decades of continuous field study, espec

The common chimpanzee (Pan troglodytes) is, with the bonobo, our closest living relative and one of the most influential subjects in the history of ethology. When Jane Goodall reported wild chimpanzees making and using tools at Gombe Stream in 1960, she overturned the assumption that tool manufacture was uniquely human. Decades of subsequent field research have shown that chimpanzees combine tool

The bonobo is one of humanity's two closest living relatives, sharing a recent common ancestor with the common chimpanzee. Found only in the lowland rainforests of the Democratic Republic of the Congo, south of the Congo River and north of the Kasai River, it occupies a single, restricted range. Once misleadingly called the "pygmy chimpanzee," the bonobo is a distinct species with a more slender b

Gorillas are the largest living primates and among the most misunderstood of our close relatives. This book focuses on the Western Gorilla (Gorilla gorilla), drawing on the well-studied Mountain Gorilla (Gorilla beringei beringei) where relevant. Popular culture painted gorillas as roaring monsters, but field study — beginning with George Schaller and Dian Fossey — shows predominantly peaceful, pl

Orangutans (genus Pongo) are the only great apes native to Asia and the largest habitually tree-dwelling mammals on Earth. Three species are recognised — the Bornean (Pongo pygmaeus), the Sumatran (Pongo abelii), and the Tapanuli (Pongo tapanuliensis), described only in 2017 and now the rarest great ape alive. All three are Critically Endangered. They live in the rainforest canopy of Borneo and Su

The lion is the only truly social member of the cat family. Where nearly all other cats live and hunt alone, lions organise their lives around the pride — a stable core of related females and their cubs, attended by a coalition of adult males that has usually immigrated from outside. This single exception to feline solitude makes the lion one of the most instructive species in animal behavior, bec

The tiger, Panthera tigris, is the largest living cat and, for ethologists, the defining example of a solitary predator whose life is organised around space rather than society. Where a wolf pack makes cooperation visible, the tiger makes land tenure visible: an adult tiger lives mostly alone and holds an individual home range, using an elaborate system of scent-marking, ground scrapes, tree-rakin

The leopard, Panthera pardus, is the most adaptable and widest-ranging of the big cats. A member of the genus Panthera alongside the lion, tiger, jaguar, and snow leopard, it occupies a greater diversity of habitats than any of them — rainforest, savanna, mountain, scrub, and semi-desert — across sub-Saharan Africa and patchily through the Middle East and South and Southeast Asia to the Russian Fa

The cheetah (Acinonyx jubatus) is the fastest land animal, and nearly everything about its behavior follows from that single specialization. A lightweight skeleton, deep narrow chest, long flexible spine that acts like a spring, enlarged heart and lungs, a long tail used as a counterbalance, and semi-retractable claws that grip the ground like cleats all serve one purpose: explosive acceleration t

The spotted hyena is one of the most misunderstood large mammals in Africa. Popular culture paints it as a cowardly, dog-like scavenger with a mad laugh, but field research reveals an intelligent, highly social predator living in large, female-dominated societies. It is one of ethology's most valuable "misread" species because almost every popular belief about it is either wrong or backwards. Taxo

The African wild dog is one of the most cooperative mammals known to science and one of the most endangered carnivores in Africa. It is not a feral domestic dog, a jackal, or a hyena, but a distinct species and the sole living member of its genus, Lycaon. The species name pictus — "painted" — refers to the mottled black, tan, white, and yellow coat that is unique to each individual. Wild dogs are

The brown bear (Ursus arctos) is the most widely distributed bear species, ranging across northern Eurasia and western North America, where its interior form is known as the grizzly and its largest coastal form as the Kodiak bear. Globally it is listed as Least Concern by the IUCN, though many small, isolated southern and European populations remain threatened. As an ethological subject, the brown

The red fox is the most widespread wild carnivore on Earth, native across the entire Northern Hemisphere and introduced to Australia, where it is a damaging invasive predator. A small member of the dog family — usually 3 to 7 kilograms, with a slender body, pointed muzzle, large ears, and a bushy tail — it thrives in an exceptional range of habitats: forest, tundra, desert, farmland, and, increasi

The domestic dog is the product of a long evolutionary partnership with humans. It descends from an extinct population of gray wolves, though the exact timing and geography of domestication are still genuinely debated and should be presented cautiously; estimates span a wide window many thousands of years ago, and proposed origins have shifted across studies. A widely discussed model holds that do

The domestic cat (Felis catus) is one of the world's most abundant carnivores and one of ethology's most instructive cases of light domestication. It descends from the African wildcat (Felis lybica), a strictly solitary, crepuscular ambush hunter of rodents across North Africa and the Near East. The cat is widely thought to have largely self-domesticated: when early farming communities began stori

The horse is a large grazing prey animal, and nearly everything about its behavior follows from that fact. Unlike a predator, it is not built to attack but to detect danger early and flee. This "flight response" — moving away first from anything sudden or unexpected, then assessing — is the horse's primary defense, supported by constant vigilance that is often shared across a group so not every an

Cattle are among the most familiar animals on Earth and among the most misjudged. The popular image of the slow, empty-headed cow is nearly the opposite of what ethology finds: a socially sophisticated herd animal with stable relationships, good memory, and a measurable emotional life. Understanding that behavior is not academic — it directly shapes the welfare of more than a billion animals. Dome

The domestic pig is one of the most behaviorally underestimated animals in the human world. Popularly linked with filth and stupidity, it is in reality a curious, socially sophisticated, and cognitively capable mammal — a domesticated form of the Eurasian wild boar (Sus scrofa) that retains almost the whole behavioral toolkit of its wild ancestor. Domesticated independently in the Near East and Ea

The domestic goat is one of the oldest and most underestimated of human livestock partners. Domesticated among the very first farm animals in the early Neolithic Fertile Crescent, roughly ten to eleven thousand years ago, it descends from the wild bezoar goat (Capra aegagrus), a mountain-dwelling caprine of the Middle East and Central Asia. Domestication did not erase the goat's ancestral characte

The domestic sheep (Ovis aries) is one of the most familiar and most misjudged animals on Earth. Everyday language treats "sheep" as a byword for mindless conformity, yet behavioural science reveals a socially intelligent grazer whose group-living strategy is an elegant answer to the problem of surviving as prey. Domesticated roughly ten thousand years ago from the wild mouflon (Ovis orientalis /

The brown rat is one of the most maligned and most scientifically important animals in the human world. Popularly cast as a filthy, solitary pest and a carrier of plague, it is in reality a highly social, cooperative, and cognitively rich rodent. A colonial burrower most likely originating in eastern Asia — despite the misleading name "Norway rat" — it now lives on every continent except Antarctic

The house mouse is one of the most widely distributed mammals on Earth and, at the same time, the single most important mammalian model in biomedical science. This dual status makes it uniquely valuable to ethology, because it forces a clear distinction between the behavior of the animal itself and the behavior of the standardized laboratory tool we have made of it. Originally a steppe rodent of c

The European rabbit is one of the most instructive prey animals in ethology and one of the most misunderstood companion animals. It is the only truly colonial lagomorph — the single rabbit or hare that lives in permanent, socially organized underground warrens — and it is the wild ancestor of every domestic rabbit breed. Native to the Iberian Peninsula and nearby areas, it has been carried by huma

The common bottlenose dolphin (Tursiops truncatus) is a toothed whale — a cetacean, not a fish — found in temperate and tropical waters worldwide, from coastal bays to the open ocean. It is one of the most socially and cognitively complex mammals in the sea, and studying it has reshaped how ethologists think about animal society, communication, and culture. Bottlenose dolphins live in fluid "fissi

The orca, or killer whale (Orcinus orca), is the largest member of the dolphin family, Delphinidae, and a global apex predator found in every ocean from the poles to the tropics. Despite the name "killer whale," it is a dolphin — the biggest one there is — and there are no confirmed records of a wild orca killing a human. Its true significance in ethology lies not in its power but in its social an

The humpback whale is a large baleen whale — a filter feeder that strains small schooling fish and krill from seawater through fringed keratin plates — found in every major ocean. It belongs to the rorqual family, whose expandable throat pleats allow enormous mouthfuls of prey-laden water to be engulfed and then pushed back out through the baleen. Adults reach roughly 12 to 16 meters, and the spec

The common octopus (Octopus vulgaris) is a soft-bodied cephalopod mollusc and one of the most cognitively striking invertebrates on Earth. Because it evolved sophisticated behavior along a molluscan line separated from vertebrates by hundreds of millions of years, it offers ethology a natural experiment in how intelligence, perception, and coordinated action can arise more than once, in a radicall

The common cuttlefish (Sepia officinalis) is a coastal cephalopod of the eastern Atlantic, Mediterranean, and North Sea — a mollusc, not a fish, in the same class as octopuses and squid. It is best known for carrying what may be the most sophisticated dynamic display system in nature in its skin. Three layers work together: chromatophores (muscle-controlled pigment sacs that switch colored spots o

The great white shark (Carcharodon carcharias) is a large mackerel shark (family Lamnidae) and one of the ocean's most studied apex predators. Despite a fearsome public image, its documented behavior is precise, exploratory, and flexible rather than mindlessly aggressive. It ranges nearly worldwide in temperate coastal and offshore seas, with well-known concentrations off South Africa, Australia,

The Nile crocodile is Africa's largest crocodilian and one of the most instructive animals in ethology because it combines extreme predatory power with genuinely elaborate parental care. It is a sit-and-wait ambush predator: rather than chasing prey, it positions itself where prey must come — a drinking spot, a river crossing, a fish run — and waits, showing little more than eyes and nostrils abov

The king cobra is the world's longest venomous snake, commonly reaching 3 to 4 metres and occasionally exceeding 5. Despite its name it is not a true cobra of the genus Naja; it belongs to its own genus, Ophiophagus, within the elapid family of front-fanged venomous snakes. The genus name means "snake-eater," and it captures the animal's defining trait: the king cobra is a dietary specialist that

The Komodo dragon is the largest living lizard and a striking example of island gigantism. It is a monitor lizard (family Varanidae), not a dinosaur or a crocodilian, and it is endemic to a handful of small, dry islands in the Lesser Sunda chain of Indonesia — Komodo, Rinca, Flores, Gili Motang, and a few islets. Large adults reach roughly 2.5–3 meters and 70–90 kilograms. On these islands, with n

The green sea turtle (Chelonia mydas) is a long-lived marine reptile in the family Cheloniidae, found in tropical and subtropical seas worldwide and assessed globally as Least Concern on the IUCN Red List (downlisted from Endangered in 2025 after decades of recovery, though several regional subpopulations remain threatened). In ethology it is best known for natal homing: females that hatched on a

Poison dart frogs, the family Dendrobatidae, are small, often brilliantly coloured frogs of the tropical forests of Central and South America. Their vivid colours are not decoration but a warning: an example of aposematism, the strategy of advertising a genuine defence so that predators learn to stay away. The defence is real toxicity, held in the frogs' skin — yet, remarkably, the frogs do not ma

The axolotl is a salamander — an amphibian, not a fish — that matters to biology less for its behavior than for its extraordinary developmental biology. Native only to the high-altitude lake system of the Valley of Mexico, it now survives in the wild solely in the remnant canals and wetlands of Xochimilco near Mexico City, where it is listed as Critically Endangered. Its defining trait is neoteny,

The Western honeybee is among the most intensively studied animals in behavioral science, and it earns that attention by challenging our idea of what a "behaving unit" is. A honeybee colony is not just a crowd of insects; it is a tightly integrated society of tens of thousands of individuals that forages, defends itself, regulates its temperature, reproduces, and makes decisions with no central co

Ants are a family of more than 14,000 species of eusocial insects that live, almost everywhere on land, in colonies organised around a reproductive division of labour. One or a few queens lay the eggs, while large numbers of sterile female workers forage, build, care for the brood, and defend the nest. Because reproduction and maintenance are split this way, biologists treat the colony as a supero

Termites are eusocial insects that, despite the old nickname "white ants," are not ants at all. They are essentially social cockroaches, now placed within the order Blattodea, and their society evolved independently of the ants, bees, and wasps — a classic case of convergent evolution. About 3,000 species live across the world's tropics and warm regions, where most are quiet, essential decomposers

The praying mantis is a common name for the insect order Mantodea, roughly 2,400 species of predatory insects found on every continent except Antarctica. Its most famous feature — the folded, prayer-like forelegs — is not a devotional or restful pose but the cocked, spring-loaded ready position of its raptorial forelegs, which snap shut on prey in a few hundredths of a second. The mantis is a sit-

The dragonfly (suborder Anisoptera, order Odonata) is one of the most accomplished aerial predators on Earth and one of the most instructive animals in ethology. With roughly 3,000 species worldwide, breeding wherever there is fresh water, dragonflies are obligate carnivores at every life stage. The adult is a short-lived (weeks to months) flying hunter; the aquatic nymph stage, which can last fro

The monarch butterfly is one of the most studied insects in ethology because it performs an intercontinental migration that no single individual completes. Each autumn, monarchs in eastern North America fly south to a small cluster of oyamel fir forests in the mountains of central Mexico, while the western population gathers at coastal California sites. The butterflies that make this journey have

Jumping spiders (family Salticidae) are the largest of all spider families, with more than 6,000 species found on every continent except Antarctica. They break nearly every assumption people hold about spiders. Rather than sitting in a web and waiting, they are active, daylight, visually guided hunters that stalk mobile prey and take it with a calculated leap — behaving far more like a cat than an

The emperor scorpion is one of the largest scorpions in the world, a heavy, glossy-black arachnid of the humid forests of West and Central Africa. Despite its formidable appearance — broad, muscular pincers and a stinger-tipped tail arched over the back — it overturns nearly every assumption its looks invite. Its venom is comparatively mild, its temperament relatively calm, and its most remarkable

The fiddler crab is one of ethology's clearest natural laboratories for studying sexual selection, animal communication, visual ecology, and biological timekeeping. Roughly 100 small species of the family Ocypodidae — long grouped as genus Uca and now split into genera such as Leptuca, Austruca, Minuca, and Gelasimus — live on tropical and warm-temperate intertidal mudflats, sandflats, salt marshe

The American lobster (Homarus americanus) is a large, clawed marine crustacean of the cold northwest Atlantic and one of ethology's clearest models for how animals build and, remarkably, remember a social order. It is a benthic, mostly nocturnal scavenger-predator that shelters in rocky crevices and burrows by day and forages the seafloor at night, eating crabs, mussels, clams, urchins, worms, sma

Squid are fast, jet-propelled cephalopods that live in open water and, in many species, in groups — a striking contrast to their solitary, bottom-dwelling relative the octopus. This book focuses on two well-studied examples: the Humboldt or jumbo squid (Dosidicus gigas), a large pack-hunting predator of the eastern Pacific, and the market squid (Doryteuthis opalescens, formerly Loligo), a smaller

The red kangaroo (Osphranter rufus, formerly Macropus rufus) is the largest living marsupial and the signature large mammal of the Australian arid interior. It is a macropod — a "big-footed" marsupial group that includes kangaroos, wallaroos, and wallabies — and lives across the dry inland plains, scrub, and grassland of mainland Australia. The IUCN Red List rates it Least Concern: it is abundant

The meerkat is a small mongoose of southern Africa's arid Kalahari region and one of the most intensively studied social mammals in behavioural science. It lives in cooperative groups — often called mobs or gangs — of roughly three to thirty individuals that share a burrow system, defend a territory, and depend on one another to survive. A lone meerkat cannot both forage with its head down and wat

The three-toed sloth (genus Bradypus) is one of the clearest examples in nature of behavior shaped by an energy budget. Rather than surviving through speed, strength, or cleverness, it survives by spending as little energy as possible. This single principle explains almost everything about how it lives. The sloth is an obligate folivore — it eats leaves, which are low in energy and protein, high i

The Common Raven is the largest of the world's songbirds and one of the most cognitively studied animals outside the great apes. Entirely glossy black, with a heavy bill, shaggy throat feathers, a deep croaking call, and a wedge-shaped tail in flight, it ranges across almost the whole Northern Hemisphere and occupies habitats from Arctic tundra to desert to city edge. The IUCN Red List classifies

The carrion crow is an all-black corvid of Europe and eastern Asia and one of the most successful large birds living alongside humans. A member of the crow family, it combines a generalist diet, a flexible temperament, and a large brain relative to its body size. Its name reflects its scavenging, but it eats almost anything: invertebrates, small vertebrates, eggs and nestlings, grain, fruit, carri

The New Caledonian crow (Corvus moneduloides) is a medium-sized, all-black crow found only on the Melanesian island of New Caledonia and its immediate neighbors in the southwest Pacific. Unremarkable at a glance, it is arguably the foremost tool-maker in the bird world and one of the most intensively studied animals in comparative cognition. Unlike animals that simply pick up a found object, this

The African grey parrot is the animal that forced science to rethink what a bird's mind can do. Native to the lowland and moist forests of West and Central Africa, it is a medium-large grey parrot with a red tail, feeding on fruit, seeds, nuts, and mineral clay, and living for several decades. It is diurnal and intensely social, moving in noisy flocks built around strong, long-term pair bonds, and

The scarlet macaw is a large, brilliantly colored parrot of the Neotropical lowlands, ranging from southeastern Mexico through Central America and across the Amazon Basin. A member of the parrot order (Psittaciformes) and the large macaw genus Ara, it lives in humid tropical forest, gallery forest, and forest edge, feeding mainly on seeds, nuts, and fruits. It is globally listed as Least Concern b

The budgerigar (Melopsittacus undulatus) is a small parrot of arid inland Australia and, in captivity, one of the most popular pet birds in the world. Weighing only about thirty grams and endemic to the semi-arid interior, it is a grass-seed specialist whose behaviour is built around the unpredictability of its environment. Rainfall in central Australia is patchy and irregular, so budgerigars do n

The rock pigeon (Columba livia) is among the most scientifically important — and most unfairly dismissed — birds in the study of animal behavior. The wild ancestor nested on sea cliffs across Europe, North Africa, the Middle East, and Asia; through domestication and escape, feral Columba livia now lives on every inhabited continent, overwhelmingly in cities. The IUCN Red List rates the species Lea

The Mourning Dove is one of North America's most abundant and widespread birds, a slender grayish-brown columbid of open country, farmland, and suburbs recognized across the continent by its soft, mournful cooing. That plaintive coo-OO-oo-oo-oo is the male's perch-coo, an advertising song used to hold a small breeding territory and attract a mate — and it is so owl-like in tone that it is the sing

The barn owl is one of the most important animals in the history of sensory biology, because it turned a night hunter's basic problem — catching a mouse it cannot see — into a solved neuroscience question. A barn owl can strike a moving rodent in complete darkness using hearing alone, displaying arguably the most acute directional hearing measured in any animal. Its pale, heart-shaped face is a pr

The Eurasian Eagle-Owl is one of the largest owls in the world and, across much of Eurasia, the apex predator of the night. A big bird — up to about 70 centimetres tall with a wingspan approaching 1.8 metres — it hunts in darkness with feet and talons powerful enough to crush and carry prey as large as a hare, and it does something unusual among birds: it regularly kills and eats other predators,

The golden eagle is the most widely distributed eagle in the world, a powerful predator of open and mountainous country across North America, Europe, North Africa, and Asia. It is a "booted" eagle of the genus Aquila, with feathers running down its legs to the toes — an adaptation for gripping and killing live prey on the ground, in contrast to the fish-catching and scavenging sea-eagles. Large, d

The bald eagle is a large North American sea-eagle famous as a national symbol but far more interesting as an animal than as an emblem. Behind its proud image is an efficient opportunist that feeds by whichever route costs the least effort. It catches fish, but it also scavenges carrion heavily and pirates catches from ospreys, gulls, and other birds — the food-stealing habit, called kleptoparasit

The peregrine falcon is the fastest animal on Earth, a distinction it owes to a single specialised behaviour: the hunting stoop. Climbing above its prey, the falcon folds its wings and drops in a steep, controlled dive that can exceed 300 kilometres per hour, converting height into overwhelming speed to overtake a flying bird before it can react. This makes the peregrine an unusually clear example

The red-tailed hawk is the most widespread and familiar large hawk in North America, and one of the clearest examples in raptor ethology of success through generalism. It belongs to the genus Buteo — the broad-winged, soaring "buzzard-hawks" — and its whole body plan is tuned not for speed but for cheap, sustained flight on rising air. That design suits an opportunist that scans wide areas of open

The secretary bird is one of ethology's most instructive cases of a predator whose lifestyle diverges sharply from its ancestry. It is a genuine bird of prey — a member of the order Accipitriformes, kin to hawks, eagles, and Old World vultures — but it has committed almost fully to a terrestrial, cursorial life on the open grasslands and savannas of sub-Saharan Africa. Standing over a metre tall o

The Indian peafowl (Pavo cristatus) is a classic example of sexual selection in birds, where the male's elaborate train is thought to have evolved through female mating preferences rather than for survival. Exactly how strongly, and by which cues, present-day peahens use the train in mate choice remains an active scientific debate — some studies report clear preferences for elaborate trains, while

The domestic chicken (Gallus gallus domesticus) is the world's most abundant bird, descended from the Red Junglefowl of Southeast Asia domesticated at least 4,000–8,000 years ago. Initially kept for cockfighting and ritual, chickens are now bred for eggs and meat on an industrial scale, with billions kept in human care worldwide. Domestic chickens show tremendous morphological and behavioral diver

The Wild Turkey (Meleagris gallopavo), a large North American ground bird, nearly disappeared from existence through overhunting but has undergone one of the most dramatic wildlife recoveries, now numbering over 4 million across North America. The species is famous for the male's elaborate display — fanned tail, iridescent plumage, and loud gobble — but equally fascinating is its complex social li

The Ostrich (Struthio camelus) is the world's largest and heaviest living bird, standing up to about 2.8 meters tall; large males can weigh well over 100 kilograms, with females somewhat smaller. Despite being flightless, ostriches are among the fastest two-legged runners on Earth, capable of sustained speeds of 50+ km/h and bursts to 70 km/h. Native to African savannas, grasslands, semi-deserts,

The Emu (Dromaius novaehollandiae) is Australia's largest bird, a flightless ratite weighing 40–60 kilograms and standing 1.5–2 meters tall. Unlike the polygamous ostrich, emus form monogamous pairs with a remarkable parental division of labor: males do all brooding and chick-rearing while females may depart before or shortly after hatching. Emus are highly nomadic, traveling vast distances across

The Emperor Penguin (Aptenodytes forsteri) is the largest living penguin and one of the most cold-adapted of all birds, breeding on Antarctic fast ice through the polar winter when temperatures fall below negative 40 degrees Celsius and winds can exceed 100 kilometers per hour. Its breeding cycle is the reverse of most seabirds: rather than nesting in the food-rich summer, emperors pair and lay in

The Wandering Albatross (Diomedea exulans) is among the largest flying birds and holds the greatest wingspan of any living species, with the largest individuals approaching or slightly exceeding 3.5 meters. It is a bird of the open Southern Ocean, spending most of its life at sea and returning to remote sub-Antarctic islands only to breed and molt. Its biology is defined by two linked themes: mast

The Herring Gull (Larus argentatus) is a large, powerful gull of Northern Hemisphere coasts and inland waters that has become one of the most visible examples of a wild bird thriving alongside people. Historically a cliff- and island-nesting seabird, it now also breeds on rooftops in towns and cities and forages heavily at harbors, landfills, and streets. This shift makes the species a compelling

The Brown Pelican (Pelecanus occidentalis) is a large coastal waterbird of the Americas, instantly recognizable for its enormous bill and throat pouch and for its dramatic plunge-diving hunt. Spotting fish from the air, the bird folds its wings and drops into the sea, using the impact to help stun prey before scooping water and fish into its expandable pouch, then draining the water and swallowing

The Great Cormorant (Phalacrocorax carbo) is a large, dark, fish-eating waterbird found across Europe, Asia, Africa, and Australia, in both coastal seas and inland lakes and rivers. It is a specialist underwater pursuit predator, diving from the surface and propelling itself mainly with its large webbed feet to chase and seize fish, which it swallows whole. Its wide distribution and its close, oft

The Greater Flamingo (Phoenicopterus roseus) is the largest flamingo species and one of the most specialised birds in the world. It belongs to its own order, Phoenicopteriformes, and occupies saline and alkaline lakes, lagoons, and salt pans across parts of Africa, southern Europe, the Middle East, and Asia. The IUCN lists it as Least Concern, but this global status conceals the vulnerability of i

The Mute Swan (Cygnus olor) is one of the largest and heaviest flying birds, a member of the waterfowl family Anatidae. Native across temperate Europe and Asia and widely introduced elsewhere, it inhabits calm fresh and brackish waters, often close to people. It is chiefly herbivorous, reaching submerged plants by up-ending on its long neck. The IUCN lists it as Least Concern, though its introduce

The Greylag Goose (Anser anser) is a large grey goose of the family Anatidae, the wild ancestor of most domestic geese, breeding across temperate and northern Europe and Asia and wintering to the south. It is a grazing herbivore of wetlands, grasslands, and increasingly farmland, and the IUCN lists it as Least Concern, with many populations growing. Its lasting fame comes from science: it was Konr

The Mallard (Anas platyrhynchos) is the archetypal dabbling duck and one of the most widespread birds in the Northern Hemisphere, the ancestor of most domestic ducks and an adaptable generalist that thrives from wild marshes to city parks. It is a medium-large member of the family Anatidae, strongly sexually dimorphic — the green-headed drake and mottled brown female — and listed by the IUCN as Le

The Common Kingfisher (Alcedo atthis) is a small, brilliantly coloured bird whose entire biology is organised around one demanding specialisation: catching fish by plunge-diving. Roughly sparrow-sized but with a large head and long dagger bill, it is widespread across Europe, Asia, and North Africa wherever there is clear, fish-bearing fresh water and earth banks for nesting. The IUCN lists it as

The great spotted woodpecker, Dendrocopos major, is a medium-small pied woodpecker of the family Picidae, widespread across the Palearctic in temperate and boreal woodland, parks, and gardens from Britain to Japan. It is a broad omnivore — taking wood-boring insect larvae, other insects, conifer seeds and nuts, and seasonally the eggs and nestlings of other birds — and it is assessed as Least Conc

The ruby-throated hummingbird, Archilochus colubris, is a tiny member of the family Trochilidae (order Apodiformes) that weighs only about 3 to 4 grams yet sustains one of the highest metabolic rates of any vertebrate. It is the only hummingbird that regularly breeds in eastern North America, and it migrates to Mexico and Central America for winter. Its diet is nectar plus small insects and spider

The European nightjar, Caprimulgus europaeus, is a nocturnal and crepuscular aerial insectivore of the family Caprimulgidae, breeding across Europe and into Asia on heathland, forest clearings, and young plantations, and wintering in sub-Saharan Africa. It feeds on flying insects, especially moths and beetles, and is assessed as Least Concern on the IUCN Red List, though it has declined locally wi

The common cuckoo, Cuculus canorus, is a medium-sized migratory bird of the family Cuculidae, breeding across Europe and temperate Asia and wintering in sub-Saharan Africa. Adults are insectivores with a notable specialisation on hairy, toxic caterpillars that most birds avoid. The species is assessed as Least Concern on the IUCN Red List, though it has declined in parts of its range. The cuckoo i

The great hornbill, Buceros bicornis, is a very large, long-lived canopy bird of the family Bucerotidae, inhabiting wet and moist forests of the Indian subcontinent, Southeast Asia, and Sumatra. It is predominantly frugivorous — figs are a staple — with some animal prey taken opportunistically, and it is topped by a massive bill bearing a large, mostly hollow casque. On the IUCN Red List it is ass

The toco toucan is the largest toucan and the owner of the largest bill, relative to body size, of any bird. That bill has been the focus of more than a century of debate, and its story is the heart of the species' ethological interest. Far from being the heavy, clumsy weapon it appears, the bill is a lightweight structure of keratin over a bony, air-filled frame, and it serves several functions a

The satin bowerbird is the classic living demonstration of sexual selection through female choice. A mature male, glossy blue-black with a violet eye, clears a patch of forest floor in eastern Australia and builds a bower: two parallel walls of woven sticks forming an avenue, with a court decorated by carefully arranged objects that are overwhelmingly blue — feathers, flowers, berries, and, near p

The superb lyrebird is a large, ground-dwelling Australian songbird and probably the most accomplished vocal mimic known. In the moist forests and fern gullies of southeastern Australia, males clear display mounds on the forest floor and, through the austral winter breeding season, sing long, complex songs while throwing an ornate tail forward over the body in a shimmering visual display. The village weaver is a small, abundant, and intensely social African bird whose males are among the most skilful nest-builders in the animal world. Using strips of grass and leaf torn and carried in the bill, a breeding male threads, loops, and knots an enclosed, hanging nest with a woven entrance, and he builds many such nests within a colony, often crowded together in a single tree. The European starling is one of the world's most successful and most controversial birds — at once the author of the breathtaking murmuration, a gifted vocal mimic, and one of the most notorious invasive species on Earth. A medium-sized, glossy, spotted songbird native to Eurasia and North Africa, it is a habitat generalist strongly associated with humans, thriving in farmland, grassland, and citi

The house sparrow (Passer domesticus) is a small Old World sparrow (family Passeridae) whose life is bound to human settlement. Native to Eurasia and North Africa, it has been carried by people to the Americas, Australia, southern Africa, and beyond, making it one of the most widely distributed wild birds on Earth. It is a commensal of humans — a wild animal that exploits the grain, food waste, an

The zebra finch (the Australian bird, now widely treated as Taeniopygia castanotis following a 2016 taxonomic split; much older literature uses T. guttata) is a small, sociable Australian grassfinch of the family Estrildidae, roughly 10 to 12 grams, with males distinguished by chestnut cheek patches, spotted flanks, and a red-orange bill. Native to the arid and semi-arid grasslands of mainland Aus

The domestic canary is the domesticated form of the wild canary (Serinus canaria), a small finch of the family Fringillidae native to the Atlantic islands of Macaronesia — the Canary Islands, Azores, and Madeira. The wild bird is a modest greenish-yellow and grey finch; centuries of selective breeding have produced the familiar bright-yellow pet canary along with many colour, type, and song breeds

The European robin (Erithacus rubecula) is a small, russet-breasted chat of the family Muscicapidae, widespread across Europe and into western Asia and North Africa. Despite its shared English name, it is not closely related to the American robin (Turdus migratorius), a thrush. It is largely insectivorous, common, and assessed as Least Concern. Beloved as a tame garden bird — famous for following

The barn swallow (Hirundo rustica) is one of the most widespread birds on Earth, a graceful, fork-tailed aerial insectivore in the family Hirundinidae. It breeds across much of North America, Europe, Asia, and North Africa and winters in the Southern Hemisphere and tropics, giving it one of the largest ranges of any passerine. Assessed as Least Concern, it is nonetheless in regional decline in som

The common swift (Apus apus) is a small, sooty-brown, scythe-winged bird of the family Apodidae, more closely related to hummingbirds than to the swallows it superficially resembles. It breeds across Europe, North Africa, and temperate Asia and winters in sub-Saharan Africa, and it is currently assessed by the IUCN as Least Concern, though several regional populations have declined. The grey crowned crane (Balearica regulorum) is a tall, grey-bodied African crane of the family Gruidae, crowned with a distinctive spray of golden feathers and marked with white and red facial patches. It lives in the wetlands, marshes, and grasslands of eastern and southern Africa, feeds omnivorously on seeds, insects, and small animals, and is long-lived, capable of surviving for several decade

The white stork (Ciconia ciconia) is a large, white-and-black wading bird of the family Ciconiidae, with a long red bill and red legs, standing about a metre tall. It breeds across Europe, North Africa, and western and central Asia and migrates to sub-Saharan Africa for the non-breeding season. The African sacred ibis (Threskiornis aethiopicus) is a medium-sized white wading bird of the family Threskiornithidae (the ibises and spoonbills), recognisable by its bare black head and neck and its long, down-curved black bill. It lives in wetlands and their margins across sub-Saharan Africa, feeding on a broad, opportunistic diet — insects, worms, crustaceans, molluscs, amphibians, fish, repti

The griffon vulture (Gyps fulvus) is a large, pale sandy-brown Old World vulture of the family Accipitridae (the hawks, eagles, and Old World vultures), with a wingspan of two and a half metres or more, a downy head and neck, and a distinctive neck ruff.
